1.blurb: Muhammad of Ghor's Ghurid invasions and his decisive 1192 victory over Prithviraj Chauhan at the Second Battle of Tarain are the immediate prelude to the Delhi Sultanate. Aibak was Muhammad of Ghor's slave-general, and Muhammad's assassination at Damyak in 1206 created the political vacuum into which the Mamluk dynasty stepped.

Mahmud of Ghazni's seventeen raids into India between 1001 and 1027 demonstrated that Hindu north India was militarily vulnerable to organized Turkic cavalry and supplied the architectural model — the Ghazni victory minarets — that Aibak echoed in the Qutb Minar two centuries later.

The Iron Pillar of Delhi was originally erected during the Gupta Empire and bears a Sanskrit inscription dedicating it to a king conventionally identified as Chandragupta II. Its presence in the Quwwat-ul-Islam mosque courtyard is the most direct physical link between Sultanate Delhi and the Gupta golden age that medieval India had inherited from.

Genghis Khan was acclaimed Great Khan of the Mongols at the kurultai on the Onon river in 1206 — the same year Qutb-ud-din Aibak proclaimed himself Sultan at Lahore. Two of the most consequential thirteenth-century empires were proclaimed in the same year, on opposite sides of Eurasia, by figures who never met but whose successors would soon collide on the Indus.

The Mali Empire, founded by Sundiata Keita in West Africa around 1235, was the contemporary African Islamic empire that ran in parallel with the early Sultanate. The two were the great non-European Islamic states of the thirteenth century, and Mansa Musa's hajj a century later would become the most famous diplomatic statement of medieval Islamic wealth.

The Ayyubid Sultanate that Saladin founded in Egypt in the 1170s — and that ruled Egypt and Syria during the Sultanate's founding period — was the principal Islamic power facing the Crusader states whose 1204 sack of Constantinople marked the same political moment in Mediterranean history.

2.blurb: Genghis Khan personally pursued the Khwarazmian prince Jalaluddin Mingburnu to the Indus river in 1221, briefly bringing the Mongol army within striking distance of Delhi. The Sultanate's survival depended on Iltutmish wisely refusing to provide shelter — and on Genghis's reconnaissance reports concluding that the climate and terrain made northern India not worth conquering.

Balban's Persianate court ceremonial — the elaborate sijdah and paibos prostration, the Zilullah ('shadow of God on earth') theology — was deliberately modeled on the Sasanian Persian court, the most ceremonialized court in the eastern Islamic memory. The Sultanate borrowed the institutional vocabulary of pre-Islamic Persian kingship through the same Persianate cultural channel that had carried it through the Abbasids and Ghaznavids.

The Mamluk Sultanate of Egypt was founded in 1250 by another set of slave-soldiers, twenty years after Aibak's Indian sultanate. The Mamluks of Egypt and the Mamluks of Delhi were the two great slave-soldier dynasties of the thirteenth century — and would later be the only two Islamic states to successfully resist Mongol absorption.

The cross-civ comparison for Razia Sultana's female sovereignty runs through the very thin list of women who held formal sovereign rule in pre-modern empires. Hatshepsut ruled Egypt as pharaoh in the fifteenth century BCE — the most prominent ancient analogue, separated from Razia by nearly three thousand years and by completely different theological and political frameworks for legitimating female rule.

The Yuan Dynasty of Kublai Khan was being established in China during Balban's Sultanate — the Mongol conquest of Song China was completed in 1279, the same decade Balban was breaking the Chihalgani's institutional power at Delhi. The two great Eurasian Mongol-affected polities of the late thirteenth century were both reorganizing the political settlements they had inherited from prior dynasties.

3.blurb: The Chagatai Mongol invasions Alauddin Khalji defeated were operating in the same period the Ilkhanate (the Mongol successor state in Iran) was peaking under Ghazan Khan. The Sultanate's anti-Mongol defense was the eastern bookend of the same century-long Eurasian struggle that produced Ain Jalut in Egypt, the Ilkhanate's own Islamic conversion under Ghazan, and the eventual disintegration of the unified Mongol world-empire.

The Mamluk Egyptian victory over the Mongols at Ain Jalut in 1260 was the western counterpart to Alauddin Khalji's six victories over Chagatai invasions between 1296 and 1306. The Sultanate and Mamluk Egypt were the only two Islamic states to successfully resist Mongol absorption — the precondition for the survival of distinct Persianate Islamic culture in both regions.

Mansa Musa of Mali was the contemporary African Islamic ruler whose 1324 hajj produced perhaps the most famous demonstration of medieval Islamic wealth — distributing so much gold in Cairo that the Egyptian markets crashed for a decade. Mali's gold-based wealth and the Sultanate's land-revenue-based wealth were the two great non-Mediterranean Islamic economic systems of the early fourteenth century.

The Ottoman beylik was founded by Osman I in northwestern Anatolia in 1299 — the same decade Alauddin Khalji was defeating the great Mongol invasions at Delhi. The Ottoman dynasty that would mirror the Sultanate's later trajectory across the next four centuries was being founded the same decade the Sultanate hit its first imperial peak.

Marco Polo, the Venetian merchant whose travels in Asia included the Yuan court of Kublai Khan and reports from south India during the same period as Malik Kafur's southern raids, was writing his memoir during Alauddin Khalji's reign. Polo's accounts of south Indian wealth provide one of the few European-language eyewitness sources for the same Hindu kingdoms — Yadava, Kakatiya, Pandya — that Kafur was raiding.

4.blurb: Ibn Battuta, the Moroccan traveler who served Muhammad bin Tughlaq as a qadi for eight years, also visited Mali two decades later (1352–1353) and left the most detailed eyewitness account of Mansa Musa's empire. His Rihla is the unique medieval source that connects the Sultanate court at Delhi and the Mali court at Niani in the firsthand observations of a single observer.

The Bahmani Sultanate that broke away from the Tughlaqs in 1347 was the first major Muslim kingdom to establish itself in India outside Delhi's authority — ending the precedent that Muslim political power in India flowed from a single Persianate imperial center. The Bahmani elite that gravitated to Persian and Arab Shia immigrants in particular created direct cultural ties to the broader Islamic world.

The Vijayanagara empire founded in 1336 by the re-converted brothers Harihara and Bukka was a deliberate Hindu counterweight to Sultanate northern expansion. It built directly on the southern Hindu temple-and-state tradition the medieval period had developed (Cholas, Hoysalas, Pandyas) and would be the dominant southern Indian power for the next two centuries until Talikota in 1565.

Akbar, who would refurbish Firuz Shah's West Yamuna Canal among many other Sultanate-era inheritances, would also be the Mughal contemporary of Suleiman the Magnificent and Selim I — running broadly parallel projects of consolidating Persianate-Islamic gunpowder empires across the Indian and Mediterranean halves of the early modern Islamic world.

The Black Death that struck Europe and the Mediterranean Islamic world in 1346–1353 — devastating Byzantine, Mamluk, and European populations — probably also reached parts of north India during the late Tughlaq period through the same Central Asian trade routes. The famines and epidemics Barani records under Muhammad bin Tughlaq may include the first Indian impacts of the same pandemic.

5.blurb: Timur — the Turco-Mongol warlord who claimed Genghisid descent and rebuilt a substantial portion of the Mongol world-empire in the late fourteenth century — wrecked Delhi in 1398 as part of the same campaign system that defeated the Mamluks at Damascus, the Ottomans at Ankara, and the Golden Horde across the Volga. The Sultanate's destruction was one regional moment of the broader Timurid reconstitution of the Mongol-Islamic world.

Mehmed II's capture of Constantinople in 1453 ended the Byzantine Empire two years after Bahlul Lodi captured Delhi from the last Sayyid sultan in 1451. The mid-fifteenth century is the inflection point at which centralized fiscal-military Islamic gunpowder states (the Ottomans, the late Sultanate, soon the Mughals and Safavids) replaced the older medieval political order across the Mediterranean and Iranian world.

King Sejong's invention of the Hangul alphabet in 1443 was the contemporary Korean cultural project of vernacular literacy — exactly the period Hindavi was emerging as a written literary register in Sultanate India. The two transitions to vernacular writing systems were independent but both reflected the broader fifteenth-century shift from elite-classical to popular-vernacular literary culture.

Zheng He's seven great voyages of the Ming Treasure Fleet between 1405 and 1433 reached the Indian port of Calicut, the Persian Gulf, and the East African coast — bringing Chinese imperial sea-power into direct contact with Indian Ocean trading networks during the same decades the Sayyid Sultanate was holding a hollowed-out Delhi. The world Babur would invade was already a globally connected world.

The Bengal Sultanate's wealth and cultural sophistication during the fifteenth century — including patronage of Bengali-language literature and the construction of the great Adina Mosque — drew directly on the broader Islamic Golden Age tradition that the Sultanate-era expansion had connected India to. Bengal under the Ilyas Shahis was one of the wealthiest Islamic states in the world.

6.blurb: The same Sufi tariqa tradition that produced the Chishti dargahs of Sultanate Delhi produced the Safaviyya order in Ardabil that became the Safavid dynasty of Iran. Both were responses to the institutional vacuum left by the Mongol disruption of the Abbasid order — Sufism filling the spiritual and political space the older centralized religious authority had occupied.

Moinuddin Chishti was originally from Sistan in eastern Iran — part of the same Persianate Sufi world from which the Safaviyya order would emerge two centuries later in northwestern Iran. The Chishti tradition's distinctive emphasis on charity, music, and openness to non-Muslim disciples set it apart from many Iranian Sufi orders, but the underlying tariqa structure was shared across the Persianate Islamic world.

The northern Bhakti tradition that produced Kabir, Mirabai, and Tulsidas in the late Sultanate built on the original Tamil Bhakti movement of the seventh through tenth centuries, when the Alvars and Nayanars had developed the template of vernacular devotional poetry directly addressed to a personal deity, accessible to all castes. The northern Bhakti movement was the same template applied to new vernacular languages and a new religious environment.

The Sufi tariqa tradition that flowered in Sultanate India had been incubated in the Abbasid period, when the great Sufi systematizers — al-Junayd, al-Hallaj, al-Ghazali — had developed the theological and institutional vocabulary that the later tariqa orders inherited. The Chishti masters were drawing on a four-centuries-old Abbasid-era spiritual lineage when they founded their Indian dargahs.

7.blurb: Babur, the Timurid prince who conquered Delhi in 1526, was Ismail I's contemporary and competitor in the same northwest Iranian and Central Asian space the Safavids were claiming. The Safavid-Mughal relationship began as geopolitical competition between two contemporary Timurid-descended dynasties before developing into the most culturally fertile bilateral relationship of the early-modern world.

Krishnadevaraya's Vijayanagara empire was the direct continuation of the southern Hindu temple-state tradition that medieval India had developed across the Cholas, Hoysalas, and Pandyas. At its peak under Krishnadevaraya, Vijayanagara was unambiguously the largest single state in the subcontinent, dominating regions the Sultanate had only ever briefly raided.

The Ottoman Janissary system of slave-soldier infantry equipped with matchlock firearms and field artillery is the same military template Babur's Turkish gun-master Ustad Ali Quli would deploy at Panipat — and that the Sultanate's heavy-cavalry-based military system had no experience defending against. The Ottoman tactical model defeated the Sultanate by proxy through Babur's borrowed application.

8.blurb: Babur's victory at Panipat in 1526 was the Indian counterpart to the Ottoman victory over the Safavids at Chaldiran in 1514 — both moments when the gunpowder army (matchlock infantry plus field artillery, deployed behind a wagon-fort defensive perimeter) defeated a traditional pre-gunpowder cavalry-based military system. The same technological transition was reshaping every major Eurasian battlefield in the early sixteenth century.

Suleiman the Magnificent's defeat of Hungary at the Battle of Mohacs in 1526 was the European counterpart to Panipat the same year — same gunpowder-revolution dynamics, same outcome of a smaller Ottoman-trained gunpowder army defeating a larger pre-gunpowder defending army. The mid-1520s are the global pivot point at which the new military technology transformed every major Eurasian battlefield simultaneously.

Suleiman the Magnificent ruled the Ottoman Empire at the same moment Babur founded the Mughal Empire (1526) — the two greatest gunpowder Islamic empires of the early modern world emerging within months of each other. The Ottoman, Safavid, and Mughal trio (the 'gunpowder empires') would dominate Islamic Eurasia from this point through the eighteenth century.

The Inca Empire under Huayna Capac was at peak in 1526 and would be destroyed by Pizarro's Spanish gunpowder army at Cajamarca in 1532 — six years after Babur's Panipat. The cross-continental synchronicity is striking: Babur and Cortes and (a few years later) Pizarro all demonstrated the same dynamic, of small gunpowder-equipped invading armies defeating much larger pre-gunpowder defending populations.

Mirza Ghalib, the great early-nineteenth-century Persian and Urdu poet of late-Mughal Delhi, was writing in a tradition that ran continuously back through Amir Khusrau six centuries earlier — the same Persianate literary tradition that Safavid Persia had also inherited from the Timurid court at Herat. The shared Persian literary world of late-medieval and early-modern South Asia and Iran was a single cosmopolitan culture.

Babur's proclamation as Padshah at Delhi in April 1526 was the founding moment of the Mughal Empire — the dynasty that would inherit, modify, and refine nearly everything the Sultanate had built. The Mughal Empire TL picks up the story from the other side: Babur's Ferghana origins, the Khyber crossing, and the gunpowder revolution that broke the Lodi army at Panipat.
